title: How to Treat Hormonal & Stubborn Acne (Gently but Effectively)
description: Breakouts that won't quit — especially along the jaw and chin? Here's a gentle-but-effective acne routine that clears skin without wrecking your barrier, plus when it's time to see a dermatologist.
painQuote: My hormonal acne keeps getting worse no matter what I try — help!
intro: Stubborn acne — deep, tender bumps around the jaw, chin, and neck that flare on a cycle — is one of the most frustrating skin problems. The instinct is to attack it with everything harsh, which usually makes it angrier.
The routine that actually works is gentle but consistent: one proven active, a non-stripping cleanser, and a barrier you don't destroy. Here's how to build it.

body: Scrubbing, drying alcohol toners, and stacking three actives at once strip the barrier — which triggers more oil and more breakouts. Red, peeling, still-breaking-out skin is the classic over-treatment loop.
Effective acne care is gentle and repetitive, not aggressive.
Adapalene (a gentle OTC retinoid) is the gold-standard starting point — it clears and prevents breakouts and smooths texture. Use a pea-sized amount at night, starting every other night.
Azelaic acid pairs well for calming redness and fading the marks acne leaves behind, and it's gentle enough for most skin.
A lightweight cleanser and an oil-free moisturizer keep the barrier intact so the active can work. A non-comedogenic sunscreen prevents the dark marks breakouts leave behind from deepening.
Yes — acne-prone skin still needs daily moisturizer and SPF.

If breakouts are deep, painful, scarring, or clearly hormonal and not responding after 8–12 weeks, see a dermatologist. Prescription options and hormonal treatments exist and work — topicals can only do so much.
This guide is general information, not medical advice.

faq.q: Adapalene or benzoyl peroxide — which should I start with?
faq.a: Both work. Adapalene (a retinoid) is great for whiteheads, texture, and prevention; benzoyl peroxide targets the bacteria in inflamed pimples. Many people use adapalene at night and a benzoyl peroxide wash in the morning, but introduce one at a time.
Give a routine 8–12 weeks. Retinoids often cause an initial 'purge' in the first few weeks before improvement — that's normal; don't quit early.

faq.q: Can topical products fix hormonal acne?
faq.a: They help a lot, but truly hormonal acne sometimes needs a dermatologist for prescription or hormonal treatment. If topicals aren't enough after a few months, book an appointment.
